author: @matthiasvaillancourt8877
text: Hi sir, I actually worked a lot with carbon fiber and made a lot of heating gloves (up to 20w per hand), the main issue to me is when the carbon fiber gets wet or humid, that 18v source, you will feel it and experience like a tiny electrocution, very uncomfortable. Your tape idea is a good step, I used latex, one coat of 0.5mm, I put the carbon strips, then one coat on top, and you have nice strips. You can use ferrule for connection, the best thing I could find!

author: @chrishayes5755
text: Funny how I asked about this kinda stuff on a forum full of electrical engineers and they gave me long, convoluted talks about how I should never attempt to make a personal heater without spending years mastering ohms law and studying 1000 page electrical textbooks. Meanwhile in a few minutes Robert gives me the exact barebones, functional information I need. Thanks again sir for all you do, you're a different breed of thinker, appreciate you 👍

replies.author: @TommyLBP
replies.text:
@chrishayes5755 the internet is full of people telling you that things can’t be done. I remember when I wanted to grow a little bit of MJ in a 4 cubic foot space under my desk. Any time I asked for advice 99% of the responses were telling me the light isn’t going to be strong enough to grow anything, that it’ll be too close to the plant and burn it, that it won’t have enough airflow, that there won’t be enough root space, ect… I solved every single one of of those issues. 

People often learn that there is a proper way of doing things, but not taught why that is. So when someone comes along with a different way of thinking they don’t have the skills to understand how these new ideas can also work.

author: @MrFriskyWhiskey
text: I don't understand how it's an 'Infra Red Heater'. The only thing I saw being 'Infra Red' was the IR Thermometer Gun.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the energy given out is IR - just because you can't see it by naked eye doesn't mean it isn't there mate - you can certainly feel it

replies.author: @MrFriskyWhiskey
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Thank you. I'm struggling to understand, for instance how a current being passed through doesn't electrocute you. I understand there is;
* electrical heat (say from electric heater - think 'toaster' for bread/crumpets)
* kinetic heat (say from friction)
* chemical/thermal heat (from a bonfire - combustion)
* Infra Red heat (say from the sun)
* Microwave heat (from a microwave/device)
Or, is all heat just 'Infra Red' heat?
My understanding of IR was that you couldn't see it, as it's invisible to the naked eyes, although it doesn't heat the surface of an object but actually 'penetrates' the object and heats it from within? Hence why IR heaters are used by automotive bodyshops for 'curing' paint and by reptile tanks.

author: @markg5891
text: Interesting! But if you'd use it to heat yourself then this is at most 50% "heat efficient". Half of the heat is simply radiated away from your body. Is there a way to sort of apply an infrared radiator (or thermal isolator) in this context? You'd need to have a material that does reflect heat back into your body but doesn't cause a short circuit and is still wearable. An iron man suit, however cool, is not an option 😏

author: @1N2themystic
text:
Man, you keep giving me ideas. This has probably become my favorite channel. I watch almost every new video I see in my feed. Thanks again. 
   As a motorcyclist, I could see making some clothing, socks, thermal underwear, etc. If 12v is enough to get some warmth, it could be plugged right into the bikes power. If you want a few more volts, I  assume you can just wind a small induction coil and add it to your circuit. 
   I really need to get my old college textbooks out and review the stuff. 
  I'm curious if you did any tests to find out the wattage per foot, per degree of temperature increase, and I wonder how long an 800amp car battery would power say a 12 foot section sewn into a sleeping bag and still have a decent amount left to start an American V8... I realize there a lot of variables. I would just be curious as to a best guess.

author: @simongross3122
text:
Really good mate.  Now I can use a method other than telling porky pies to set my pants on fire :)

I think a great use for these is portable heat packs for therapeutic use.  No more overcooking wheat packs in the microwave.  Just stick a battery in it.  Or even for heated blankets.  No more plugging them into the mains and hoping nobody spills a cup of tea over you.  Heated bedding for pets.  And so on.

I'm going to try to make electric heat packs this way.  We often need them for various aches and pains.

Well done.  Extremely simple and endlessly useful.

author: @Ludotooki
text: Considering the recent dogma of heating the individual being better than heating the environment, it makes me wonder. I've priced out the costs for getting a 10,000 mah battery pack, along with a foldable perovskite/ETFE style backpack camping solar cell and a large coat with several heating units set up in much this way. It came out to around $100 all told, but that's for retail prices and retail products. If that cost could shrink, I feel it could make a real dent in preventing suffering and perhaps even deaths from cold and winter for homeless, for starters, beyond being a far more flexible way to camp outdoors!

author: @princenameless
text:
Can anyone help me please figure out my heating issue? I would appreciate your opinion. 

The hallway outside my flat front door is incredibly warm. I sometimes open the door to get some of that hot air in. 

Could I make something covert that would let the heat in? Essentially a stationary hit sink fitted to my door. Looks like a regular number plate. 

Do you think simply drilling a hole in the door, putting a copper plate on the front door, painting the door number on it, with a bit of copper plumbing in the cavity of the door with another copper plate on the back of the door would it work in transferring some of the heat from the hallway, so I can have my door shut and still benefit from the heat? Does the copper plate on the front of the door need to be bigger than the back of the door [inside the flat]? 

It’s at-least x10 degrees warmer in the hallway, actually... you begin to perspire in the hallway if You hang about too long in layers.  Do you think it would work as an idea? I think it could but I’m not sure of the most efficient way. If the copper/brass plate on the front of the door should be larger than the inside plate, if they should be equal in size.

replies.author: @steelcityriddims
replies.text: I think a little fan fitted in the door could work well in your situation. The ones you find in desktop computers should do the job.

author: @pikotech1
text:
Thanks Rob, another great infrared video! 

If I remember correctly, standard solar cells for panels generate around 5 Watts each (you can buy "B grade" factory "faults" for next to nothing).

So, have a bunch of those running into a few batteries throughout the day, and make some carbon fibre wallpaper. Sure, you won't be looking at huge temperatures, but it will hold a steady temperature keeping the room above silly temperatures during the evenings. And best of all, it can probably be done for a couple of hundred pounds, and will never need refueling.

It's on my radar for a test project. If I get round to it I'll share the results.

author: @colfaxschuyler3675
text:
Question: at 2:20, you mention it's drawing 1.1 ma - is that correct?  I would think it would be 1.1 A (amps) for about 10 W
Otherwise, it would be 0.01 Watts, which sounds very low for a heating element.

replies.author: @colfaxschuyler3675
replies.text:
Noticed that further in, you do mention Amps, so it was just a mis-speak.

Question, would you address the length vs the current use?  With the example of the LiIon battery pack: if you doubled the length of the element, it should be a lower heat, but last twice as long.
I haven't worked with carbon fiber, but Ohm's law should still be valid, unless there something weird with how the resistance shows up because of multiple/ parallel fibers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no - mis speak that I corrected late - but you spotted it lol - cheers

replies.author: @colfaxschuyler3675
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Can you talk to the IR emission spectrum?
I've been studying photobiomodulation and there are specific spectral bands that more active biologically, and others that mainly excite water and appear as sensible heat

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: Carbon fiber provides a better greater surface area, but is more expensive than nichrome wire and the latter can handle higher temps without degrading.  For something like gloves--a fairly small amount of material needed, yeah, I would probably use the carbon fiber. That way, you could even get wider cloth and cut out a mitten or glove shape of the carbon fiber cloth and get a really even, full coverage of heat. (If you do that, best to tape off or silicone caulk the edges of the cloth so that it stays together better. It unravels fairly easily, especially in wider cloths).

author: @justinw1765
text:
Appreciate the video in the general, but think traditional insulation works plenty well for most conditions and is far more efficient (cost, time, resource etc wise). Carbon fiber isn't cheap (and is fairly dirty environmentally). Batteries aren't cheap.  Electricity is becoming less cheap. But things like kapok fiber are cheap and do a really good job of slowing energy/heat loss from a body. 

     If I was doing an Antarctica trip during their summer  and had an integrated pulk shelter with solar panels on the roof, then maybe I would add in some carbon fiber heating strips to my clothes, sleeping bag, or the like.  

   But then again, I could just as easily line the pulk shelter with vacuum insulated panels* and bring some beeswax candles or the like and be ok. 

  * (someone might say, well VIP's are kind of expensive. Yeah, commercial ones are, but not so expensive if you know how to make them yourself at home).

replies.author: @karlmyers6518
replies.text:
I have everything here to make carbon fibre heater. 
Care to show me how to make VIP's and insulation?

replies.author: @kennethbain4290
replies.text: How DO you make VIP's ? They sound interesting!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nothing wrong with a good old bit of insulation mate

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
​@kennethbain4290   I learned from a combo of researching and thinking holistically about it.  For the home version, you need a few things:  You need a *commercial grade* vacuum food sealer--one that can pull 28" Hg. You need mylar/foil lined vacuum packaging bags (the regular, non foil lined ones won't hold a vacuum more long term). These need to be a thicker variety, least 4mm. 

    Then you need some kind of spacing, insulative material for inside.  Industry most commonly uses these three--fumed silica (even without a vacuum pulled, this material is very insulative--similar to silica aerogel), perlite, and fiber glass batting. Combos of different materials have been experimented with and used as well. 

    You can use other materials. If using fibrous material, hollow fibers (but that are open on at least one end) are much better than solid. Heck, you could even put bamboo poles in if you knock out the culms and drill some small holes to help the air to evacuate.  (You would still want to combine it with one or more of the above, for the space above and below where the bamboo poles touch). 

   Then you need to put getters in there before you evacuate and seal it all up. The plastic part of the bags will both off-gas and allow some air (especially oxygen) and/or moisture to permeate through over time.  The getters absorb and/or adsorb most of this to keep the vacuum more of a, well vacuum, longer term.  

   There is a wide range of getters used, but one of the simple, cheaper, and fairly effective ones is activated carbon.  Then, typical oxygen absorbing packets can be used in combo.  There are more expensive and exotic getters used. Tbh, I know less about this part of it. 

   The best vacuum insulated panels made by industry are rated to last up to around 40/50 years or so.  Expect a home made one like the above to probably last from around a decade to 25 years or so, unless you really put some good research and thinking into the materials used.  Or how you approach it as a system.  For example, you can put the vacuum sealed pouch/panel in an all foil bag to provide further protection.  You won't be able to suck out all of the air in that most likely, but by getting rid of most of the air and having an extra, all metal barrier between the inner pouch and the ambient atmosphere, it will help to prolong the life and slow down these processes of gas permeation. Aluminum incidentally is one of the best materials for holding a vacuum.  

   Industry VIP's can also be under a slightly harder vacuum than the 28" Hg.  But 28" Hg imo is sufficient to reach a pretty good insulation capacity.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
​@karlmyers6518   As to VIP's, see above. Regular insulation is pretty easy. Warm socks, hats, sweaters, gloves, jackets etc if you can't make them yourself. People that wear shorts and t-shirts inside that complain about the cold, well I have no patience for them.  Used to have a housemate/tenant like this.  Told him to wear a sweater or shut up. 

     Kapok fiber is a great, inexpensive insulation if one can sew, either by hand or by machine.  If you can sew by machine and have one, then some nylon tulle acts as a good baffle material.  You take a piece of tulle that is wider than the fabric you are sewing it to, and then you sew like "channels" of the tulle onto the other fabric, to which later you will stuff the fiber into.  

     Then after, you sew  your other regular fabric to the regular fabric below the tulle. Voila, you have an insulated fabric. 

     I don't recommend kapok fiber for larger items in connection with specifically backpacking though. Goose down is superior, both in insulation value and especially in compressibility.  Kapok fiber doesn't compress as much (which is great for pillows, cushions, etc) and so it's not as a good for sleeping bags, or even backpacking used jackets. Makes awesome mittens, insulated hoods, insulated booties though, especially since it is so hydrophobic and keeps it loft far better when damp and wet as compared to down (which collapses more). 

    Pre the invention and common use of synthetic foams, kapok fiber was used in life vests, Arctic boots, etc because it can help a piece float in water, its highly insulating, and highly hydrophobic.  It is also far cheaper than goose and even lower quality duck down.

author: @stevem7868-y4l
text: Please do a vid, on which we should use, Gas or Electicity to heat our homes, my place is gas heating, but with electric pumps, or should i be heating individual rooms that are occupied with an electric heater ? i ask you, as you are much more in tune with this stuff than i am,

replies.author: @sandpiperetching4248
replies.text: We have no gas in our village so I have individual heaters in the rooms.  I actually think it is better than central heating as you can warm the rooms you want to.  I have a separate water heating tank

replies.author: @thisorthat7626
replies.text: Rob mentioned that it is more important how the heater heats than the source of heat.  Conduction, radiation or convection heat.   Electric infrared heaters in the occupied room are very efficient.  Gas heating often heats the air in the room thru convection, which is very inefficient.   Heating one room is more efficient than heating rooms that aren't being used if the same type of heating is used.   He talks more about this in his previous videos on heating.  Cheers.

replies.author: @sandpiperetching4248
replies.text: @ThisorThat  I have an infrared heater in my bedroom which I put on shortly before going to bed.  An electric blanket too.  Unfortunately a convector heater in the lounge but it is thermostatically controlled.

author: @alulaleak
text: How come heated clothing on the market can last 8hrs, while these last 15-40mins? Is it the heating material? Btw, this is awesome 👏🏽

replies.author: @joshbeaulieu7408
replies.text:
Good question. I would want to see the specs of a comparable setup to try and figure it out. He told use the voltage and amperage so we can calculate resistance for comparison. 

From there I would look at whether commercial rigs have circuits to limit power draw from the battery, lowering output but extending duration. 

The 8 hour life could only be a marketing gimmick based on use at an ultra low setting, right? 

I like the question though. There certainly are a bunch of different heating elements you could use but these materials are mostly readily available to the average viewer. 

I love these ideas!

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
Probably they are using significantly lower voltages/amps and only heating to something like 95* F or so, which is around (slightly above) the temp of outside of skin. The less draw, the longer the battery will last. If you're putting this within/below insulation, you don't really need to heat it much since the insulation will be "trapping" both the heat of the heater and of your own body.  (Trapping not the best term, more like slowing down the exchange of energy from outside to inside rather).

   Also, they are using new lithium ion batteries which have higher energy density per volume and mass. Especially as compared to a 9 volt alkaline battery or an old, probably LiFeP0, tool battery. 

    Those times may also be slightly optimistic and within best case lab conditions i.e. non real world conditions.  Bring that out into an interior Alaskan cold winter day with -40 or colder and doubt it will last as long because there will be more and faster energy exchange (or "heat loss").

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: because they don't get that hot - energy consumption and heat are related - more heat more energy less heat less energy - if you don't get it hot it will last the same time - anyway you don't want it hot - warm is good enough

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering i worry about LACKING radiation. The penetration to the joints and bones and deep muscle tissue, is the one benefit IR has over regular resistance wire mat. This was the USe of the red Philips IR lamps of the 70s onwards. My Tomado seat heater, on the other hand, clearly is a regular resistance heater.

replies.author: @joshbeaulieu7408
replies.text:
@perpetual4958 hmm, I haven't heard of in floor heating using an IR film. Sounds like a similar, but cool idea! The old way works well too, a hot water line running in the floor. 

If I'm not mistaken IR doesn't damage tissue in limited amounts and is used in medical instruments to look through your flesh for all sorts of purposes like pulse oximetry and IR therapy.

author: @jeremyevans1702
text: Do you need to tape this to keep it from shorting if you bury a couple straps in a sand battery and attach them to a solar panel of sorts?

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
Not if there isn't any way for the strip to fold in on itself and for the leads/crimps to touch each other. Both sand and air are inherently electrically insulating at these voltages and amps.  The tape is recommended if it is possible for the leads/crimps can potentially touch each other. 

     For the method you are using, I personally would use cheaper and more heat resistant Nichrome wire--probably wrapped around copper pipe. If wrapping the Nichrome wire around the copper pipe, be careful though to make sure neither the crimps/leads nor wires carrying the electricity from the source to the crimps/leads touch the copper pipe. That can also short the electrical source. To make sure this doesn't happen, you can just use silicone caulk to cover the crimps/leads. You don't need to tape the whole thing, etc.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: only if it is moving - if it is fixed by sewing or weight of sand - no need for it

replies.author: @midfin3807
replies.text: Is it for heat storing sand battery?

author: @russbeck2
text: Hi Robert, could this be wrapped around  copper pipe to preheat water going into a boiler?

replies.author: @alulaleak
replies.text: You probably want to use nichrome wire for that but be careful!

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
Yes, but you have to really shield/insulate (electrically) the conductive leads/crimps (or whatever way you are conducting electricity from the source to the carbon fiber). Copper pipe is very electrically conductive and will readily "short" the power supply if both leads/crimps are touching it. 

     Also, it won't be a very efficient way of preheating.  Water flows pretty fast through a pipe, so unless you are wrapping a rather long length of pipe and are heating up the carbon fiber to a significantly higher temp, it just won't add much heat into the water. Adding thermal insulation around it will help a bit, but unless it is a long length of pipe, pretty hot, etc again won't preheat much.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes - but put caption on the pipe first then the carbon tape with a small gap between each winding
